notes.anchor: by coming to my house and meeting with my people
notes.note: Appius had supported Cicero's bid for honours not only by speaking and voting in the Senate but by the personal courtesies of Roman patronage: calling at Cicero's house and canvassing his connections.
Cicero claims political solidarity with Appius as fellow optimates who "hold the same opinion" about the res publica, the conservative senatorial cause on the eve of the civil war.
The fresh bond of marriage-connection (adfinitas) created by Tullia's match with Dolabella, which Cicero insists has only deepened, not diminished, his devotion to Appius.

notes.anchor: as addressed to a master of public manners
notes.note: The censor exercised the regimen morum, official oversight of public and private conduct; Cicero jokes that he keeps the letter short and restrained because he is now writing to a censor.
